title: Your Clicks Cost $6.59 and 98% Bounce: Why Google Ads Bleed Contractors | Booked Job
h1: Your Clicks Cost $6.59 Each and 98% Bounce — Why Google Ads Bleed Contractors Dry
meta: The average home-services Google click costs $6.59 (LocaliQ 2025) and contractor sites convert just 2-3% (WebFX 2026). Why fixing your site beats a bigger ad budget.
short: The average home-services Google Ads click costs $6.59 (LocaliQ 2025). The average contractor site converts 2-3%, so ~98% of those paid visitors leave without contacting you (WebFX 2026). That's why a "reasonable" click turns into an unaffordable cost-per-job — and why fixing the site beats raising the budget.
statLabel: Average cost-per-click for home services on Google Ads (LocaliQ 2025)

body.html: A $6.59 click isn't the problem. A site that ignores 98% of those clicks is. The average home-services click on Google Ads costs $6.59 (LocaliQ 2025). That's not crazy. What's crazy is what happens after the click: the average contractor website converts just 2 to 3% of visitors, which means about 98% leave without contacting you (WebFX 2026). You paid for every one of them. You booked almost none of them. That gap — reasonable click, terrible conversion — is what quietly turns your Google Ads bill into an unaffordable cost-per-job. And you can't fix it by spending more.

body.h2: Do the click math nobody shows you
body.answer: Here's the arithmetic, using only real numbers.
body.html:
Here's the arithmetic, using only real numbers. Clicks cost $6.59 each (LocaliQ 2025). Your site turns 2 to 3% of them into a contact (WebFX 2026). At 3%, you need about 33 clicks to get one lead: 33 x $6.59 is roughly $220. At 2%, you need about 50 clicks: 50 x $6.59 is roughly $330. So before you've talked to a soul, one lead cost you somewhere between $220 and $330 — and that's just a lead, not a booked job. Close half of them and your cost-per-job doubles again.

None of that math changes if you raise your budget. Double your spend and you double your leads and your bill. The cost-per-job stays exactly the same, because the leak — the site — is still leaking.

body.h2: Why raising the budget makes it worse, not better
body.answer: Think of your funnel as a ratio, not a total.
body.html:
Think of your funnel as a ratio, not a total. If 98% of paid visitors bounce (WebFX 2026), pouring more money in the top just sends more people through the same leaky floor. You feel busy. Your bank account doesn't.

Now flip it. Take that same 2-3% site (WebFX 2026) and get it to convert even a little better — a clear phone number, a fast-loading page, one obvious 'get a quote' button, proof you're real. You didn't spend one extra dollar on clicks, but every click is suddenly worth more. Conversion is a multiplier on money you're already spending. Budget is not.

$6.59 is the all-home-services average (LocaliQ 2025). Some trades pay far more per lead. On Google Search, the cost-per-lead runs roughly $45 for HVAC, $52 for plumbing, $58 for electrical, $79 for roofing, and $94 for general contractors (LocaliQ 2025 / CallRail set). Read those numbers next to a 2-3% site (WebFX 2026) and the danger is obvious: the more you pay to get someone to the page, the more it hurts when the page fumbles them. A general contractor paying ~$94 a lead cannot afford a site that drops 98% of the traffic it took to earn that lead.

body.h2: Owning the phone beats renting more clicks
body.answer: 72% of contractors are increasing their marketing budgets in 2026, and 62% say lead generation is their single biggest challenge (CallRail 2026).
body.html:
72% of contractors are increasing their marketing budgets in 2026, and 62% say lead generation is their single biggest challenge (CallRail 2026). Most of them will 'fix' it the expensive way — by buying more clicks. But a click you rent is gone the second your card declines.

Compare the outcomes. Exclusive Google Local Services leads come in around $168 per booked job (SearchLight Digital 2026). Paid search clicks at $6.59 through a 2-3% site can quietly cost you $220-$330 per lead before you close anything (LocaliQ 2025; WebFX 2026). The difference isn't the ad platform — it's whether the page catches the visitor.

body.h2: Fix the leak, then talk about budget
body.html:
Speed matters too, and it's cheap. Answer fast: people who respond within 5 minutes are 100x more likely to qualify a lead than those who wait 30 (MIT Sloan 2026), 78% of homeowners hire the first contractor who responds (Lead Connect 2026), and contractors still miss 14% of inbound calls (CallRail 2026). A paid click that finally converts is worthless if the phone rings out.

So the order is simple: fix conversion before you fix budget. Make the page fast, make the phone number obvious, answer it, and prove you're trustworthy. That's the whole job at Booked Job — get found, get picked, get booked — so the $6.59 you already spend stops leaking out the bottom.

faq.q: Is a $6.59 cost-per-click too expensive for a contractor?
faq.a: The click itself isn't the problem — $6.59 is the average home-services CPC on Google Ads (LocaliQ 2025). The problem is what happens after the click. The average contractor site converts just 2-3% of visitors (WebFX 2026), so at $6.59 a click you're spending roughly $220 to $330 to get a single lead. A reasonable click becomes an unaffordable cost-per-job only because the site wastes 98% of the traffic.

faq.q: Should I raise my Google Ads budget to get more jobs?
faq.a: Not first. If your site converts 2-3% (WebFX 2026), raising the budget just sends more paid visitors through the same leak — you get more leads and a bigger bill, but your cost-per-job stays the same. Fixing conversion is a multiplier on money you already spend; raising budget isn't. Fix the page, then scale the spend.

faq.q: How much does a paid Google lead really cost a contractor?
faq.a: Using real numbers: at $6.59 per click (LocaliQ 2025) and a 2-3% site conversion (WebFX 2026), you need roughly 33 to 50 clicks per lead, which is about $220 to $330 per lead before you've even quoted the work. High-ticket trades pay more per lead on search — roughly $79 for roofing and $94 for general contractors (LocaliQ 2025 / CallRail set) — so a weak site hurts them most.

_verdict.notes: Fact-checked against content/stats.json (the sourced stats DB / moat). Every figure traces: $6.59 CPC (avg-cpc, LocaliQ 2025); 2-3% conversion + ~98% bounce (site-convert, WebFX 2026); trade Search CPL $45/$52/$58/$79/$94 (search-cpl-tier1, LocaliQ 2025/CallRail set); ~$168 LSA cost per booked job (lsa-cpbj, SearchLight Digital 2026-02); 72%/62% (budgets-up, CallRail 2026); 100x within 5min vs 30 (5min-100x, MIT Sloan 2026); 78% first responder (first-responder, Lead Connect 2026); 14% missed calls (missed-call, CallRail 2026). The derived figures — 33 and 50 clicks per lead, and $220-$330 per lead — are legitimate arithmetic on sourced inputs (1/0.03≈33; 1/0.02=50; 33×$6.59≈$220; 50×$6.59≈$330), not standalone claims. "5 minutes" and "30" are part of the MIT Sloan stat definition, not free-floating. No unsourced numbers found. Tone is blunt and on-brand ("bleed contractors dry", "You feel busy. Your bank account doesn't", "a click you rent is gone the second your card declines"). Article returned unchanged.
